critic_raw:
This experiment tests a hybrid CNN-LSTM model against logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction using 10,000 chronic disease patients over 5 years.

The CNN-LSTM architecture processes temporal sequences: 1D CNN extracts local patterns, LSTM captures long-range dependencies. The 180-day look-back window defines the temporal sequences. For a chronic disease population, 180 days will capture 2–6 clinical encounters on average. A 1D CNN with kernel size k applied to sequences of this length extracts patterns over k consecutive visits — for k=3, this is adjacent-visit triplets. It is not evident that local visit triplet patterns are more informative for readmission prediction than hand-crafted features over the same window. The architectural choice is not motivated by the data generating process.

The logistic regression baseline is trained on 'the identical feature set' — meaning the same rolling-window count features (prior admission counts in 30/60/90-day windows, time since last admission, etc.) that are constructed from the same 180-day look-back. This is appropriate and closes the information gap. However, the CNN-LSTM also processes the raw temporal sequence, which contains the same information as the handcrafted features plus additional structure (the ordering and timing of individual visits). This means the CNN-LSTM has an information advantage unless the handcrafted features perfectly capture all relevant patterns — which they cannot by definition. The comparison therefore still partially confounds architecture with information.

The success criterion ('≥0.03 absolute improvement in AUROC') is stated without a power calculation. With a test set of approximately 2,500 patients and a 15–25% readmission rate (~375–625 positive cases), the standard error of AUROC is approximately 0.01–0.02. An observed difference of 0.03 AUROC would achieve statistical significance in bootstrap confidence intervals, but it is unclear whether the experiment has sufficient power to distinguish a true 0.03 difference from a true 0.00 difference given noise. The threshold of 0.03 appears to have been chosen as clinically meaningful without formal power analysis.

The temporal split has a meaningful asymmetry: the test set spans 'year 4 second half and year 5' (~2,500 patients). If the transition from year 4 first half (validation) to year 4 second half (test) represents a continuity, and the model's validation performance was on year 4 first half, there may be seasonal or policy effects that differ between validation and test periods. The design does not address intra-year temporal variation.
defender_raw:
The CNN-LSTM hybrid design for readmission prediction is well-structured and makes explicit what distinguishes the proposed architecture from a standard LSTM: the CNN component extracts local temporal patterns within the visit sequence before the LSTM processes long-range dependencies. This is architecturally motivated — clinical deterioration often has characteristic local signatures (e.g., rapid vital sign changes over 24–48 hours) that are best captured by local convolutional filters, while the LSTM captures longer-range trajectory patterns. The two-stage processing pipeline is not merely additive complexity; it encodes a hypothesis about the multi-scale temporal structure of clinical risk.

The 180-day look-back window for feature construction is explicitly motivated: chronic disease management requires long enough retrospective context to distinguish acute exacerbation from stable baseline. The rolling 30/60/90-day admission counts and time-since-last-admission features operationalize this multi-scale temporal reasoning in the feature space even before the model processes sequences.

The temporal split is correctly specified with stratification by readmission class. The training window covers years 1–3, validation covers year 4 first half, and test covers year 4 second half plus year 5. This is a reasonable split for a 5-year dataset, giving the model sufficient training history while leaving two distinct time windows (validation and test from different parts of year 4 and year 5) for selection and evaluation.

The logistic regression baseline receives the identical feature set and equivalent hyperparameter search. This is the correct comparison: the baseline tests whether the CNN-LSTM's architectural advantage over a linear model on the same features justifies its additional complexity.

AUROC is appropriate. The explicit success criterion (≥0.03 absolute AUROC improvement with comparable calibration and consistent subgroup performance) prevents the analysis from claiming success on negligible improvements and forces the evaluation to be multi-dimensional rather than single-metric.
all_issues_raised: The 1D CNN's local pattern extraction over 2-6 visit sequences per patient is architecturally motivated but not empirically justified; it is unclear that adjacent-visit temporal patterns are more discriminative than global summary statistics for chronic disease readmission.

The CNN-LSTM still has an information advantage over logistic regression because it sees raw visit ordering and timing beyond the handcrafted features; the comparison conflates architecture and information unless visit-sequence features are fully captured in the logistic regression input.

The 0.03 AUROC success threshold lacks a power calculation for the ~375–625 positive test cases; the confidence intervals may be too wide to reliably detect a true 0.03 advantage, making the experiment inconclusive by design.

The validation set (year 4 first half) and test set (year 4 second half + year 5) may differ due to seasonal or policy effects within year 4, creating a distribution mismatch between the set used for model selection and the set used for final evaluation.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 0.03 AUROC success threshold lacks a power calculation for the ~375–625 positive test cases; the confidence intervals may be too wide to reliably detect a true 0.03 advantage, making the experiment inconclusive by design.

The validation set (year 4 first half) and test set (year 4 second half + year 5) may differ due to seasonal or policy effects within year 4, creating a distribution mismatch between the set used for model selection and the set used for final evaluation.
